comment.body:
Remember, the whole idea behind “Holy Alliance 2.0” has rested on unchallenged U.S. hegemony.
But if Trump’s calculations fail and his aggressive tariff war ends up strengthening the BRICS, Russia will no longer need the United States at the cease‑fire negotiations—because it will be the U.S. that finds itself in zugzwang if it wants to avoid an armed conflict with China.
comment.raw_body:

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
Washington’s tariffs are meant not simply to punish Beijing but to push it into a broad deal—ideally brokered through Putin, who has long experience negotiating with communist regimes.
If talks collapse and China turns openly hostile, Taiwan becomes the pressure point. But Trump has no desire to launch a war whose outcome no one can predict. 
A subtler way to limit Beijing’s reach is the Arctic corridor. Chinese ports are lucrative gatekeepers of east‑west trade, but a northern sea‑route—anchored by gas projects such as Shtokman—would lower freight costs, supply Europe with cheap Russian gas developed with U.S. technology, and bypass China altogether.

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
Washington’s strategy is to squeeze Beijing over many years, trimming Chinese involvement in global supply chains and applying varied forms of economic pressure. In the Middle East, Trump wants to be taken seriously, so he needs a partner he can trust, and this should be Putin.
Putin can mediate between Mohammed bin Salman and Trump. Gulf investors have vast capital but demand respect; through Russian channels they can bridge the gap with Washington.

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
The problem is that Trump’s policy is highly unpredictable—he is too impulsive and too impatient. You can never be sure what will happen overnight, and investors don’t invest in uncertainty; meanwhile they’re tired of intraday trading.
Trump needs to settle the dust with China somehow, because what is happening now poses a big problem for the funds and people who are supposed to support the U.S., save its ass, and build the new Holy Alliance among the United States, Russia, and the Middle East.

comment.body:
As it stands for now, the results we’re seeing do not favor the U.S. at all, and that’s the biggest issue. 
Russia and the Gulf states are already looking for (temporal) alternatives, because investors cannot remain stagnant. 
If the U.S. fixes things, they’ll come back; if not, and they lose confidence, the U.S. is lost.

comment.body:
UBS strategists note that these measures aim to lower Treasury yields and reduce the cost of U.S. debt servicing, in part by targeting foreign official holders of Treasuries.
Among the key elements are the potential withholding of interest payments and the issuance of ultra-long-dated or even perpetual bonds to selected foreign reserve holders.

comment.body: Evil Ferguson quote flys in the face of history. In the 1820’s Great Britain had a huge budget deficit of 200-250% of GDP because of the Napoleonic wars. It’s military spending was £15-20 mill and it’s interest expense was £28-30 mill. UK GDP was approx £500 mill. Yet the British empire grew thru the whole of the 19th century. I think the caveat on that on that quote probably needs to be about Gov total spending, that maybe more important. UK gov total spending in 1820’s was ££50-60 mill. So it paid the debt, maintain the military and maintained the empire for 10-12% of GDP.
comment.raw_body: Evil Ferguson quote flys in the face of history. In the 1820’s Great Britain had a huge budget deficit of 200-250% of GDP because of the Napoleonic wars. comment.body: I’m not sure you can separate the territorial issues completely from the technological. Sure China could invade Taiwan at the drop of a hat but currently they could not hold it. That won’t change until their ability to develop state of the art military technology has improved. Their development of the semi tech might be inevitable but that still doesn’t mean they can catch up within the next 10-15 years. From market perspectives we need to be estimating timelines and probabilities for these possible scenarios. China will likely remain a rational actor until the political situation in China becomes so fraught they have no choice but to make a (currently) sub optimal move like attacking Taiwan. They are playing chess without a queen at the moment imo and would much prefer to get a pawn promoted before they make their next big move. As a rational actor they should remain patient waiting for the perfect chance or until they have no choice.

comment.body: I just want to point out to folks that these huge bear leverage plays could be people shorting the ETF’s not so much for price action but because of the high volatility. Loaning These 3 x ETFs is very expensive but sometimes it makes sense because when the market is chopping about and the decay is so huge a pairs trade with SPY can pay very well even if market goes nowhere much

comment.body: Alma, friday tenors' vol has weekend vol embedded in it, while mondays' vol are almost always lowest in a week, when analyzing change of vol or skew along the tenor axis, is there a way to normalize vol to account for this "weekend effect"?
